docs.title: Spine Hyperbook
docs.text:
This Spine Hyperbook is intended as a free, curriculum-mapped, spine surgery reference for orthopaedic and neurosurgical trainees in the UK, inspired by the BOFAS Hyperbook for foot and ankle surgery.

This is an independent project

This resource is currently being built and maintained independently. It is not associated with any specialist society. The aim is to demonstrate what a shared national resource could look like.

docs.title: What this is
docs.text: Every page is mapped explicitly to a specific line in the ISCP Trauma & Orthopaedic Surgery curriculum, and, where relevant, the ISCP Neurosurgery curriculum, so trainees know exactly what depth of knowledge they're expected to have and how it will be assessed. Every page also carries a short evidence base drawn from current literature, with named trials rather than vague "studies show" claims, as well as flagging genuine ongoing controversies rather than presenting settled answers where none exist.

docs.title: How to use this site
docs.text:
• Use the top navigation to browse by anatomical region or clinical category.
  • Use Tags to filter by curriculum depth level, critical-condition status, or topic type.
  • Each page's curriculum mapping box tells you the exact page reference in the source curriculum document. See How the Curriculum Mapping Works for what the numbers actually mean and a link to the source PDF.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: See About & Contributors if you'd like to write or review a page.

docs.title: How the curriculum mapping works
docs.text: Every clinical page on this site carries a curriculum mapping box. This page explains what the numbers mean and links to the source document on the ISCP website.

docs.title: The source document
docs.text:
All curriculum references currently on this site are to the ISCP Trauma & Orthopaedic Surgery Curriculum:

T&O Surgery Curriculum, Version 3, August 2026 (PDF, hosted by ISCP)

A neurosurgical mapping layer is planned but not yet built. When it is, it'll reference:

ISCP Neurosurgery Curriculum, Feb 2026 (PDF, hosted by ISCP)

Curricula get revised, page numbers can change

Every page reference on this site (e.g. "p.53") refers to the page number printed at the bottom of the curriculum PDF, not the PDF file's internal page count, and it is not a permanent identifier. ISCP updates these documents periodically. If a citation looks wrong, check you're comparing against the same version quoted in the box, and if ISCP has published a newer version, the reference may need updating. Treat page numbers as "correct as of the version stated", not as permanently fixed.

docs.title: Reading a knowledge item: the X/Y/Z number
docs.text:
Most curriculum mapping boxes quote a pattern like 2/4/4. This is always three numbers separated by slashes, and they always mean the same three training phases, in the same order:

Position Training phase 1st number Core (CT1-2 / ST1-2, generalist surgical training before subspecialisation) 2nd number ST3-8 (all trainees, regardless of eventual subspecialty) 3rd number Specialty interest (trainees who have chosen spine as their area of focus)

docs.title: What the numbers themselves mean
docs.text: The curriculum uses two different scales depending on whether the item is a knowledge item or a hands-on skill. This site's mapping boxes are always explicit about which one applies. Here is the definition of each:

docs.title: Knowledge scale (Basic Science, Critical Conditions, Assessments, Treatments)
docs.text: Score Meaning 1 Knows of 2 Knows basic concepts 3 Knows generally 4 Knows specifically and broadly

docs.title: Skills scale (Applied Clinical Skills)
docs.text:
Score Meaning 0 No experience expected 1 Has observed, or knows of 2 Can manage with assistance 3 Can manage the whole procedure, but may need assistance 4 Able to manage without assistance, including common complications

Skills use a 0-4 scale rather than 1-4. "0" exists specifically to mark procedures a trainee at that level isn't expected to have experienced.

So 2/4/4 means: core trainees are expected to know basic concepts, while both general ST3-8 trainees and spine-interest trainees are expected to know the topic specifically and broadly.

docs.title: A worked example
docs.text:
From the Cauda Equina Syndrome page:

Knowledge level: 3 (core) / 4 (ST3-8) / 4 (specialty interest)

This is a knowledge item, using the 1-4 scale. Even core trainees (position 1) are expected to know it generally (3), not just know of it, which is a noticeably higher bar than most other spine topics carry at core level. Every trainee from ST3 onwards, regardless of subspecialty, is expected to know it specifically and broadly (4). That combination is a fair proxy for how safety-critical the curriculum authors consider the topic, and it is worth noticing the core-level number being unusually high.

Compare that with a typical elective topic, from ACDF vs Cervical Disc Replacement:

Skills: ACDF (p.66, level 0/1/4)

This is a skills item. Core trainees aren't expected to have experienced it (0). General ST3-8 trainees are expected to have observed it or know of it, but not to perform it (1). Only trainees with a declared spine special interest are expected to reach full independence, including managing complications (4). The jump straight from 1 to 4, skipping any middle ground for the general ST3-8 trainee, is itself informative: this procedure isn't something you're expected to become competent at unless spine is your chosen path.

docs.title: Assessment routes
docs.text:
Some mapping boxes also name how a topic is assessed within ISCP. The main ones referenced on this site:

  • CBD — Case-Based Discussion
  • CEX — Clinical Evaluation Exercise
  • PBA — Procedure-Based Assessment

Critical conditions are typically assessed via CBD or CEX (they're about judgement and knowledge, not a specific procedure). Skills items are assessed via PBA when a trainee logs the relevant procedure.

docs.title: If something looks wrong
docs.text: If a page number, a competency level, or a quoted curriculum line doesn't match what you find in the source PDF, that's worth flagging. Curriculum documents get revised and it's possible a citation was accurate for an earlier version and hasn't been updated. Check the version and date stated at the top of this page against the PDF you're looking at before assuming either source is wrong.

docs.title: Why this exists
docs.text: Foot and Ankle trainees have had BOFAS's Lectures of Distinction and the Hyperbook for years. Spine surgery, split awkwardly across T&O and Neurosurgery training, has had nothing equivalent. This is an attempt to build one, starting with T&O content and adding a neurosurgical layer as the project grows.

docs.title: Editorial approach
docs.text:
• Every clinical page is mapped to a specific curriculum reference.
  • Every page carries an evidence table citing named studies, not unreferenced claims.
  • Where the evidence is genuinely mixed, the page says so explicitly rather than presenting false certainty.
  • Content follows standard copyright practice: ideas and findings are summarised and attributed, direct quotation is minimal.

docs.title: How to contribute
docs.text:
1. Get in touch [add your contact route here].
  2. You don't need to know git or Markdown, a Word document is fine, it gets converted for you.
  3. If you're comfortable with GitHub, pages live as Markdown files in the docs/ folder of the repository, submit a pull request.
  4. All clinical content is reviewed before publication.

docs.title: Cervical Spine
docs.text:
Degenerative, deformity, and instability conditions affecting C1-C7. Cervical trauma is covered separately under Trauma, and inflammatory conditions affecting the cervical spine (rheumatoid, ankylosing spondylitis) sit under Inflammatory.

The two most commonly confused conditions in this section, radiculopathy and myelopathy, are deliberately kept as separate pages. They have different natural histories and different urgency, radiculopathy usually settles on its own, myelopathy usually doesn't.

  • Cervical Radiculopathy, nerve root compression, usually self-limiting
  • Cervical Myelopathy, spinal cord compression, tends to progress without treatment
  • ACDF vs Cervical Disc Replacement, a direct comparison for when either is a reasonable option
  • Craniocervical Junction & Atlantoaxial Instability

docs.title: ACDF vs Cervical Disc Replacement
docs.text:
Curriculum mapping

Skills: ACDF +/- fusion (p.66, level 0/1/4) vs cervical disc replacement (p.66, level 0/1/3) — note the lower specialty-interest ceiling on arthroplasty.

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Content outline drafted, full clinical write-up and comparison table pending.

docs.title: Evidence base
docs.text: Study Key finding Systematic review with meta-analysis, 14 RCTs, 3160 patients, Bone Joint J 2018 TDR superior to ACDF at 2 years and 4-7 years on NDI, SF-36, dysphagia, satisfaction; less adjacent segment disease. Ten-year outcomes of cervical disc arthroplasty vs ACDF, systematic review with meta-analysis, 2024 Confirms durability of TDR's advantage to a full decade of follow-up. Retrospective single-surgeon comparative study, mean 5.4 years, 2022/2023 No significant difference in complication or reoperation rate in real-world practice, a counterweight to the more favourable RCT meta-analyses.

docs.title: Cervical Myelopathy
docs.text:
Curriculum Mapping

T&O curriculum: The aging spine and degenerative disease, Basic Science > Pathology (p.52, level 2/4/4). Compressive conditions including radicular, stenotic, myelopathic, Treatments > Operative (p.53, level 1/4/4).

Skills: Cervical laminectomy, cervical laminoplasty, cervical vertebrectomy for myelopathy, ACDF +/- fusion, posterior decompression +/- fixation/fusion C2-C7 (p.66, level 0/1/4).

See also: ACDF vs Cervical Disc Replacement for the operative comparison, and Lumbar Spinal Stenosis for tandem stenosis.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: Degenerative cervical myelopathy (DCM), most commonly caused by cervical spondylosis, is the leading cause of spinal cord dysfunction in adults worldwide. Unlike radiculopathy, which is usually self-limiting, DCM tends to progress in a stepwise fashion. This is the single most important thing to communicate to patients.

docs.title: Aetiology
docs.text:
Cord compression develops from a combination of:

  • Osteophyte formation and disc-osteophyte complexes at the vertebral endplates
  • Hypertrophy of the ligamentum flavum
  • Degenerative spondylolisthesis
  • Congenital canal narrowing, symptoms often emerge when a pre-existing narrow canal combines with later spondylotic change
  • Ossification of the posterior longitudinal ligament (OPLL), more common in East Asian populations and associated with a different natural history, with a greater propensity for progression
  • Less commonly: tumour, epidural abscess, trauma, or fixed cervical kyphosis

Cord injury itself is thought to arise from a combination of direct mechanical compression and microvascular ischaemia with impaired spinal cord perfusion.

Be aware

Tandem stenosis, coexisting lumbar spinal stenosis, occurs in roughly one in five patients with cervical stenosis. It is worth having a low threshold for whole-spine imaging in a patient with mixed upper and lower limb symptoms. See the Lumbar Spinal Stenosis page.

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
Two scoring systems are routinely used in the UK, and it's worth understanding both.

Nurick grade is based purely on gait and employability. It is quick to apply at the bedside:

Grade Description 0 Root symptoms only, no cord signs 1 Signs of cord compression, normal gait 2 Gait difficulty, but fully employed 3 Gait difficulty preventing employment, walks unaided 4 Unable to walk without assistance 5 Wheelchair-bound or bedbound

Modified Japanese Orthopaedic Association (mJOA) score is the scale used in the current international treatment guidelines (see below), and scores upper limb motor, lower limb motor, sensory, and bladder function out of 18. It's more granular than Nurick and is what determines the "mild/moderate/severe" categories referenced in the AO Spine guideline.

docs.title: Clinical presentation
docs.text:
• Gait disturbance is the most common and clinically important presenting feature, patients often describe feeling unsteady rather than reporting frank weakness
  • Bilateral, non-dermatomal hand numbness and clumsiness, dropping objects, difficulty with buttons and other fine motor skills
  • Neck pain is frequently mild or absent
  • Bladder symptoms, most often urgency rather than frank retention (which is a relatively late manifestation), are of limited diagnostic value in this age group given how common incidental urinary symptoms are

docs.title: Examination findings, and how much to trust them
docs.text:
Orthobullets and similar US resources often present clonus and Hoffmann's sign with tidy single sensitivity/specificity figures. The real picture from the systematic review literature is messier and worth teaching honestly:

Sign What the evidence actually shows Tromner's sign Among the best-performing individual clinical signs in current evidence, with high sensitivity (93-97%) and specificity (79-100%), outperforming Hoffmann's sign across pooled studies (Jiang et al. 2024; Sharma et al. 2025). Worth noting the underlying studies are heterogeneous and confidence intervals are still fairly broad Hoffmann's sign Highly variable across studies, sensitivity reported anywhere from 0-94%, specificity 0-90% depending on the study population. Common, but not reliable enough on its own Babinski sign Low sensitivity (around 7-53%) but consistently high specificity (92-100%), useful for confirming, not screening Sustained clonus Similarly low sensitivity, high specificity (96-100%) Inverted supinator (inverted radial) reflex Moderate sensitivity, good specificity Hyperreflexia generally One of the more sensitive findings, but can be masked by a coexisting peripheral neuropathy (e.g. diabetic) or lumbar radiculopathy

Teaching point: sensitive signs (hyperreflexia) are generally better for screening, while specific signs (Babinski, clonus, inverted supinator) are better for confirming DCM once suspected. Current evidence suggests Tromner's sign may be the exception to that trade-off, performing well on both axes, though no sign, including Tromner's, should be used in isolation, and it's less familiar to many clinicians than Hoffmann's, worth practising deliberately rather than assuming competence.

Also examine for: Lhermitte's phenomenon (electric-shock sensation down the spine on neck flexion, not specific to DCM and also seen in multiple sclerosis and vitamin B12 deficiency), broad-based or unsteady gait, and a positive Romberg's test suggesting dorsal column involvement.

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
• MRI cervical spine is the investigation of choice. T2 cord hyperintensity reflects spinal cord injury (oedema, gliosis and/or myelomalacia), while T1 hypointensity is associated with more advanced irreversible injury and a poorer prognosis.
  • Plain radiographs have a role for assessing sagittal alignment, though degenerative changes are extremely common in asymptomatic older adults and shouldn't be over-interpreted in isolation. Flexion-extension radiographs may be useful where instability is suspected but are not routinely required.
  • CT is complementary to MRI, and is particularly useful for characterising OPLL as well as osteophyte morphology pre-operatively.
  • CT myelography is now rare, reserved for patients who cannot have MRI, or in whom MRI is significantly degraded by metal artefact (e.g. from prior instrumentation).

docs.title: Differential diagnosis
docs.text: Normal age-related gait change, stroke, vitamin B12 deficiency (subacute combined degeneration), motor neurone disease, multiple sclerosis, peripheral neuropathy, and normal pressure hydrocephalus are the differentials most likely to be confused with DCM, particularly in a patient with a mild or atypical presentation. Peripheral neuropathy and normal pressure hydrocephalus are both easy to overlook, and both commonly coexist with the same elderly population at risk of DCM.

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
AO Spine / Cervical Spine Research Society guideline (2017, still current UK reference)

  • AO Spine / CSRS clinical practice guidelines, Global Spine Journal 2017
  • Moderate or severe DCM (mJOA <14): surgery is recommended.
  • Mild DCM (mJOA >14): either surgical decompression or a supervised trial of structured rehabilitation is an acceptable option, this is a genuine choice, not a default to observe. Rehabilitation has little evidence for reversing established neurological deficit, it is used primarily to support function alongside close clinical observation rather than as a treatment expected to improve myelopathic signs.
  • Patients managed non-operatively should be offered surgery if they deteriorate.

Surgical goals are decompression of the cord and, where indicated, correction of alignment. The choice between an anterior approach (ACDF, ACDR, corpectomy) and a posterior approach (laminectomy +/- fusion, or laminoplasty) is guided mainly by the number of stenotic levels, the presence and magnitude of fixed cervical kyphosis, and where the compression is coming from (anterior pathology tends to favour an anterior approach).

Controversy: How urgent is 'urgent'?

The phrase "time is spine" is standard teaching. Earlier surgery in moderate/severe disease limits long-term disability, but mild disease it is unclear. The guideline explicitly permits a trial of rehabilitation rather than mandating surgery, which surprises trainees who assume any myelopathy is an automatic operative indication. OPLL may modify this further: some Asia-Pacific practice favours earlier or even prophylactic surgery given OPLL's tendency to progress, reflecting a higher regional incidence and correspondingly more experience with its natural history.

docs.title: Prognosis
docs.text: The primary aim of surgery in DCM is to halt further neurological deterioration rather than reverse it. Most patients experience some degree of improvement in mJOA score postoperatively, but complete normalisation is uncommon, and residual symptoms are frequent, particularly in longstanding disease or where preoperative imaging shows T1 cord signal change. This is worth setting as an expectation with patients before surgery, not just noting afterward.

docs.title: Complications
docs.text:
• C5 palsy: pooled prevalence around 5.3% after cervical decompression, slightly higher after posterior surgery (5.8%) than anterior (5.2%), and higher still in laminectomy-and-fusion specifically. The typical pattern is predominantly deltoid ± biceps weakness with minimal sensory disturbance. Onset is typically within days of surgery; most cases recover over weeks to months, though a meaningful minority don't fully resolve.
  • Pseudarthrosis: more common with multilevel constructs than single-level.
  • Dysphagia: a recognised complication of anterior approaches.
  • Postoperative kyphosis: a specific risk of laminectomy performed without fusion.

docs.title: Evidence base
docs.text: Study Key finding AO Spine / CSRS clinical practice guidelines, Global Spine Journal 2017 Surgery recommended for moderate (mJOA 12-14) or severe (mJOA <12) DCM. For mild DCM, either surgery or supervised structured rehabilitation is acceptable. Degenerative cervical myelopathy: timing of surgery, EFORT Open Reviews 2025 OPLL is a modifier of surgical timing; emerging MRI biomarkers (e.g. SIMS score) may eventually refine timing in mild disease. Brannigan J et al., single-centre service evaluation of AO Spine guideline implementation, Global Spine Journal 2024 (Cambridge) Real-world evaluation of guideline implementation in a UK unit. Mean mJOA improvement of 1.5-1.9 points at 3, 6 and 12 months post-op. Clinical signs and symptoms for DCM: scoping review, Spinal Cord 2025 Tromner's sign and hyperreflexia most sensitive; Babinski, clonus and inverted supinator most specific. No single sign reliable in isolation. The value of clinical signs in the diagnosis of DCM: systematic review and meta-analysis, Global Spine Journal 2024 Confirms the sensitive-vs-specific split above across pooled data from over 70 studies. Prevalence of C5 nerve root palsy after cervical decompressive surgery: meta-analysis, European Spine Journal 2015 79 studies, 13,621 patients. Pooled prevalence 5.3% (95% CI 4.6-6.0%), slightly higher after posterior than anterior surgery.

docs.text:
1. Fehlings MG, Tetreault LA, Riew KD, et al. A Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Patients With Degenerative Cervical Myelopathy: Recommendations for Patients With Mild, Moderate, and Severe Disease and Nonmyelopathic Patients With Evidence of Cord Compression. Global Spine Journal. 2017;7(3 Suppl):70S-83S.
  2. Fasinella MR, Benato A, Creatura D, Morgado A, Barrey CY. Degenerative cervical myelopathy: timing of surgery. EFORT Open Reviews. 2025;10(6):403-415.
  3. Brannigan JFM, Vellaiyappan SK, Mowforth OD, et al. Outcomes for Degenerative Cervical Myelopathy Following Implementation of the AO Spine International Guidelines: A Single-Centre Service Evaluation. Global Spine Journal. 2024.
  4. Sharma S, Sial A, Sima S, Diwan A. Clinical signs and symptoms for degenerative cervical myelopathy: a scoping review of case-control studies to facilitate early diagnosis among healthcare professionals with stakeholder engagement. Spinal Cord. 2025;63:171-180.
  5. Jiang Z, Davies B, Zipser C, et al. The value of Clinical signs in the diagnosis of Degenerative Cervical Myelopathy - A Systematic review and Meta-analysis. Global Spine Journal. 2024;14(4):1369-1394.
  6. Shou F, Li Z, Wang H, Yan C, Liu Q, Xiao C. Prevalence of C5 nerve root palsy after cervical decompressive surgery: a meta-analysis. European Spine Journal. 2015;24(12):2724-2734.
  7. Davies BM, Mowforth OD, Smith EK, Kotter MR. Degenerative cervical myelopathy. BMJ. 2018;360:k186. One of the most widely cited overview articles on DCM and a landmark paper for UK trainees.

Further reading

  1. Fehlings MG, Tetreault LA, Riew KD, Middleton JW, Wang JC. A Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Degenerative Cervical Myelopathy: Introduction, Rationale, and Scope. Global Spine Journal. 2017;7(3 Suppl):21S-27S. The companion paper to reference 1, useful background on how the guideline was developed and why.

docs.title: Cervical Radiculopathy
docs.text:
Curriculum mapping

T&O curriculum: The aging spine and degenerative disease, Basic Science > Pathology (p.52, level 2/4/4). Compressive conditions including radicular, stenotic, myelopathic, Treatments > Operative (p.53, level 1/4/4).

Skills: Anterior Cervical Discectomy +/- Fusion, cervical disc replacement (p.66, level 0/1/4 and 0/1/3 respectively).

See also: Cervical Spondylotic Myelopathy, a different condition with a different natural history and different urgency, worth being explicit with trainees about why these are separate pages rather than one combined topic.

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Split out from the combined radiculopathy/myelopathy page, curriculum mapping is done, full clinical write-up pending. Unlike myelopathy, cervical radiculopathy is usually self-limiting, most cases improve with non-operative management, and the evidence base and urgency framing are genuinely different, worth reflecting that difference clearly once this page is written rather than reusing myelopathy's content with the nouns swapped.

docs.title: What the page should cover
docs.text:
• Dermatomal pattern recognition and how to distinguish radiculopathy from myelopathy on history and examination alone
  • Spurling's test and its diagnostic accuracy
  • Natural history, most cases resolve without surgery
  • Conservative management pathway
  • Surgical indications and options (ACDF vs posterior foraminotomy vs disc replacement, cross-linking to the ACDF vs disc replacement comparison page)

docs.title: Evidence base
docs.text: Pending, search terms to start from: "cervical radiculopathy natural history conservative management", "cervical foraminotomy versus ACDF outcomes", "Spurling test diagnostic accuracy cervical radiculopathy".

docs.title: Evidence base
docs.text: Study Key finding Weinstein SL et al., BrAIST, NEJM 2013 Landmark RCT: bracing significantly reduced progression to surgical threshold vs observation. Stopped early on ethical grounds. Strong dose-response with wear time (>12.9h/day gave 90-93% success). Vertebral body tethering series (cited in recent AIS/AdIS review literature) One series reported 95% success avoiding fusion at 5 years. Promising but a much younger, smaller evidence base than bracing's.

docs.title: Emergencies
docs.text:
The time-critical conditions, gathered here deliberately rather than left where they'd sit anatomically, so anyone on call looking for urgent guidance doesn't have to know in advance which region of the spine is involved.

  • Cauda Equina Syndrome
  • Bilateral Facet Dislocation, a genuinely time-critical trauma presentation, and one of the best-yield FRCS (Tr & Orth) viva topics on the whole site given its unresolved pre-reduction MRI controversy
  • Spinal Epidural Abscess, the infective counterpart to the other two, most dangerous precisely because the "classic" presentation everyone's taught is actually rare

Metastatic Spinal Cord Compression is equally time-critical but lives under Tumour, see Metastatic Spinal Cord Compression rather than duplicating it here. Worth adding a genuinely comprehensive "who to call and how fast" section to this page once there's more than one condition on it.

docs.title: Bilateral Facet Dislocation
docs.text:
Curriculum mapping

T&O curriculum: Trauma module, Specific fracture areas: Spine, "The acute fracture and dislocation" (p.60, level 2/4/4) and "Spinal shock and cord syndromes" (p.60, level 3/4/4).

Skills: Trauma Spine, Cervical, anterior/posterior fixation for fracture/dislocation, application of halo/tong traction, MUA fracture/dislocation (p.66, level 0/1/4).

A classic FRCS (Tr & Orth) viva topic: it combines an acute decision-making scenario, a genuine unresolved evidence controversy (pre-reduction MRI), and a named classification system, exactly the combination examiners favour.

See also: Cervical Spine Trauma, Spinal Cord Injury, and Cauda Equina Syndrome for the equivalent timing-of-decompression debate in a different part of the spine.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: Bilateral facet dislocation (BFD) is a high-energy flexion-distraction injury of the subaxial cervical spine in which both inferior articular processes of the upper vertebra displace anteriorly past the superior articular processes of the vertebra below, "jumping" or "locking" the facets. It represents complete disruption of the posterior ligamentous complex, the facet capsules, and typically the posterior longitudinal ligament and disc, making it one of the most unstable injuries in the subaxial cervical spine. Neurological injury, ranging from root symptoms to complete tetraplegia, is common given the degree of canal compromise at the moment of injury.

docs.title: Mechanism and associated injury
docs.text: Distraction combined with flexion is the classic mechanism, most often from road traffic collisions, diving into shallow water, or falls. Because bilateral facet dislocation requires disruption of the entire posterior tension band plus the disco-ligamentous complex, a concurrent traumatic disc herniation is common, this is the anatomical basis for the central management controversy below.

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
Distinguish bilateral (both facet joints dislocated at one level) from unilateral facet dislocation (a rotational injury, usually less unstable, with a lower rate of complete cord injury). Within bilateral injuries, facets may be perched (articular surfaces resting on top of one another, partial dislocation) or locked/jumped (fully displaced, articular surfaces facing each other, mechanically more stable in the displaced position and typically harder to reduce closed).

The Subaxial Cervical Spine Injury Classification (SLIC) score, developed by the Spine Trauma Study Group, is the most widely used system for subaxial injuries generally and is worth being fluent in in this context:

Category Finding Points Morphology No abnormality 0 Compression 1 Burst 2 Distraction (facet perch, hyperextension) 3 Rotation/translation (facet dislocation) 4 Disco-ligamentous complex Intact 0 Indeterminate 1 Disrupted 2 Neurological status Intact 0 Root injury 1 Complete cord injury 2 Incomplete cord injury 3 Ongoing cord compression in a patient with a neurological deficit +1

A total score of 4 or less generally favours non-operative management, 4 is indeterminate, and 5 or more favours surgery. A bilateral facet dislocation with any neurological deficit will typically already score well into the surgical range from morphology and neurology alone. Worth knowing for the viva: SLIC's inter-rater reliability for the disco-ligamentous complex component specifically was only moderate in the original validation study, which is part of why the AO Spine subaxial classification has since been developed as a more hierarchical, better-validated successor, useful if an examiner pushes on the limitations of SLIC rather than just its content.

docs.title: Clinical presentation
docs.text: Neck pain and deformity following high-energy trauma, with a wide spectrum of neurological findings from normal through radiculopathy to complete tetraplegia below the injury level. Assess and document neurological status thoroughly and immediately on arrival, this baseline matters enormously both for management decisions and later medicolegal scrutiny.

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
CT is the investigation of choice for characterising the bony injury and facet position. MRI defines the disco-ligamentous complex, any traumatic disc herniation, and the degree of cord compression or intramedullary signal change, but when to obtain it relative to reduction is the central controversy in this topic.

Controversy: is pre-reduction MRI necessary before closed reduction?

This is one of the longest-running unresolved debates in spine trauma, and it centres on one landmark study whose own lead author later described the safety question as still "unclear."

  • Vaccaro et al., Spine 1999: a prospective study of 11 awake, neurologically intact or incomplete patients undergoing awake closed traction reduction, with MRI both before and after. Disc herniation was identified in several patients before reduction, yet none deteriorated neurologically after reduction, including patients whose disc herniations were not addressed surgically first. This became the primary evidence cited for proceeding to closed reduction without routine pre-reduction MRI in an awake, cooperative, examinable patient.
  • Multiple subsequent case reports describe patients who deteriorated neurologically after closed reduction performed without pre-reduction imaging, attributed to retropulsion of herniated disc material into the canal during the reduction manoeuvre, the scenario pre-reduction MRI is specifically intended to prevent.
  • A survey of the Spine Trauma Study Group's own members found highly variable practice regarding both the timing and influence of MRI on management, confirming this remains a genuine, unresolved practice variation among specialists, not merely a historical debate that's since been settled.
  • Working position, and the actual examinable distinction: awake closed reduction in a cooperative, examinable, neurologically intact or incomplete patient, with the reduction attempted under direct neurological monitoring and abandoned immediately if the patient deteriorates, is supported by the original evidence base without routine pre-reduction MRI. Patients who are obtunded, intubated, intoxicated, or otherwise not neurologically examinable during the procedure cannot provide this safety monitoring, and for this group pre-reduction MRI to exclude a significant disc herniation is the more widely supported approach. The distinction is examinability during the reduction, not just "is a herniation present."

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Closed reduction in the awake, examinable patient is typically attempted first: serial cervical traction using a halo ring or Gardner-Wells tongs, with weight added incrementally (historical series describe safely using up to 140 lb in a graded fashion under close neurological monitoring) while repeating neurological examination between increments and stopping immediately if any deterioration occurs. Sedation is used cautiously, since it can mask the neurological monitoring the whole technique depends on.

Open reduction is indicated when closed reduction fails, when the patient cannot be safely examined during the attempt, or when a significant disc herniation has been identified and needs addressing before or during reduction. An anterior approach is classically favoured when there is a concurrent disc herniation requiring discectomy, though a combined anterior-posterior approach is often needed given the degree of posterior ligamentous disruption typically present in a genuinely bilateral, locked injury.

Controversy: does timing of decompression change neurological outcome here?

This mirrors the timing debate on the Cauda Equina Syndrome page closely enough that it's worth drawing the parallel explicitly for viva purposes, complete with its own published disagreement between authors.

  • STASCIS, Fehlings et al., PLoS One 2012: the largest prospective multicentre study on this question, comparing early (<24h) versus late (≥24h) decompression after traumatic cervical spinal cord injury. The unadjusted analysis found a significantly higher proportion of patients achieving at least a 2-grade AIS improvement at 6 months in the early group.
  • This finding was directly challenged in a published letter to the editor by van Middendorp, who reanalysed the raw data presented in the paper and reached a different conclusion about the statistical strength of the association, another example, like the CES timing literature, of a headline "time matters" finding being contested on methodological grounds by another specialist rather than being straightforwardly settled.
  • A subsequent AO Spine clinical practice guideline (2017) nonetheless recommends decompression within 24 hours where feasible for acute traumatic cervical spinal cord injury, reflecting that the balance of evidence, imperfect as it is, still favours earlier surgery.
  • Working position: aim for decompression, whether by successful closed reduction or surgery, within 24 hours where the patient's overall condition allows, while recognising the underlying trial evidence for this specific cut-off has been directly disputed by at least one specialist reanalysis, useful nuance for a viva answer that wants to show awareness beyond "time is spine."

docs.title: Complications
docs.text:
• Neurological deterioration during reduction, the central risk this whole topic is organised around.
  • Vertebral artery injury, worth considering with any cervical facet injury, particularly with a rotational/translational component.
  • Failure of closed reduction, more likely with locked/jumped facets than perched facets, and more likely the longer reduction is delayed from injury.
  • Construct failure or non-union after stabilisation, relevant to the anterior-only versus combined approach decision above.

docs.title: Evidence base
docs.text: Study Key finding Vaccaro AR et al., Magnetic resonance evaluation of the intervertebral disc, spinal ligaments and spinal cord before and after closed traction reduction, Spine 1999 11 awake patients, pre- and post-reduction MRI. Disc herniation present in several patients did not preclude safe awake closed reduction under neurological monitoring. The landmark study underpinning "no routine pre-reduction MRI in the examinable patient." Grauer JN et al., The timing and influence of MRI on the management of patients with cervical facet dislocations remains highly variable, Journal of Spinal Disorders & Techniques 2009 Survey of Spine Trauma Study Group members found wide variation in practice, confirming this remains a live, unresolved controversy among specialists. Vaccaro AR et al., The Subaxial Cervical Spine Injury Classification System (SLIC), Spine 2007 Derivation and validation of the SLIC score. Raters agreed with the algorithm's treatment recommendation in 93.3% of cases; disco-ligamentous complex component had only moderate inter-rater reliability. Fehlings MG et al., STASCIS, PLoS One 2012 Multicentre prospective cohort. Decompression within 24 hours of traumatic cervical SCI associated with significantly higher odds of 2+ grade AIS improvement at 6 months in the unadjusted analysis. van Middendorp JJ, Letter to the editor regarding STASCIS, The Spine Journal 2012 Reanalysis of STASCIS's own raw data reaching a different conclusion about the strength of the timing association, a genuine published disagreement, not a later study overturning an old one.

docs.text:

  2. Grauer JN, Vaccaro AR, Lee JY, et al. The timing and influence of MRI on the management of patients with cervical facet dislocations remains highly variable: a survey of members of the spine trauma study group. Journal of Spinal Disorders & Techniques. 2009;22(2):96-99.

  4. Cotler JM, Herbison GJ, Nasuti JF, Ditunno JF Jr, An H, Wolff BE. Closed reduction of traumatic cervical spine dislocation using traction weights up to 140 pounds. Spine. 1993;18(3):386-390.

docs.title: Cauda Equina Syndrome
docs.text:
Curriculum Mapping

T&O curriculum: Critical Condition, Spine module (p.53) and Appendix 3 master critical conditions list (p.89).

Knowledge level: 3 (core) / 4 (ST3-8) / 4 (specialty interest), even core trainees are expected to know this generally, not just know of it.

Assessment route: CBD or CEX, to level 4 by certification (Appendix 3, p.89).

Related item: History and examination of the painful and injured spine including special clinical tests (Assessments, p.53, level 3/4/4).

See also: Metastatic Spinal Cord Compression, the other time-critical spinal condition, and Lumbar Disc Herniation & Sciatica for the more common, non-emergency presentation this sits at the severe end of.

The GIRFT National Suspected Cauda Equina Syndrome Pathway

Everything on this page should be read alongside the GIRFT National Suspected Cauda Equina Syndrome Pathway, developed with over 60 UK health professionals and periodically updated (February 2023, interactive version October 2023, most recently March 2026). This is the standard of care a patient with suspected CES in the UK should expect to receive. See also the interactive version, designed for point-of-care use on any device.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Cauda equina syndrome (CES) is compression of the lumbosacral nerve roots below the level of the spinal cord (typically below L1-L2), causing a characteristic combination of bladder, bowel, sexual, saddle sensory and lower limb motor/sensory disturbance. It is one of the few true orthopaedic surgical emergencies and one of the highest-volume sources of litigation in UK spinal practice. CES affects roughly 1-3 people per 100,000 population in England, with around 8,000 suspected cases assessed each year (GIRFT).

Most suspected CES is not CES

In the largest prospective UK cohort to look at this directly (Hoeritzauer et al., Neurology 2021), 69% of patients referred with suspected CES had no radiographic evidence of cauda equina compression on MRI, and only around a quarter had confirmed compression, consistent with an earlier systematic review's estimate of roughly 19%. A related Oxford analysis has separately reported that over 80% of urgent MRIs requested for suspected CES show no compression (SuCESS score development, BJS 2026). This matters for how the whole topic should be taught: the diagnostic challenge isn't just recognising CES, it's managing a high-volume pathway where true positives are a minority, without either over-scanning everyone or missing the genuine emergencies. The ongoing CESER study is specifically trying to improve this, recruiting 2,000 patients with suspected CES to identify which symptoms predict a confirmed diagnosis.

docs.title: Aetiology
docs.text:
• Central lumbar disc prolapse (most common)
  • Spinal stenosis with superimposed disc prolapse
  • Tumour, primary or metastatic
  • Epidural haematoma or abscess
  • Trauma
  • Iatrogenic, post-surgical haematoma or direct injury

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
There is no single internationally agreed definition of CES. A systematic review of 110 studies, 52,008 patients (Mustafa et al., PLOS ONE 2023) found that only 14.5% used an established definition at all, the rest used study-specific criteria, most commonly built around urinary dysfunction, perianal sensory change, and reduced anal tone in varying combinations. Where a named definition was used, the Fraser criteria and the BASS definition were the most common, followed by Gleave and MacFarlane.

The most widely used classification is the two-stage Gleave and MacFarlane system. Gleave and MacFarlane's original classification defined only CESI and CESR. "CES-Suspected" is a later, widely used clinical addition (including in the GIRFT pathway) to capture patients before bladder involvement.

Category Features CES-Suspected Back pain with bilateral radiculopathy, no bladder/bowel symptoms CES-Incomplete (CESI) Altered urinary sensation, loss of desire to void, or poor stream, without frank retention, bladder still under executive control CES-Retention (CESR) Painless urinary retention with overflow incontinence, bladder no longer under executive control CES-Complete Complete, established sensory and motor loss

The transition from CESI to CESR is generally considered to reflect a step-change in the degree of nerve root injury, and therefore carries prognostic weight, though the underlying pathophysiology remains debated and the point of transition can be hard to pin down precisely.

docs.title: Clinical presentation
docs.text:
• Bilateral or alternating sciatica
  • Saddle (perianal) sensory disturbance
  • Bladder dysfunction, most commonly urgency, altered sensation or a poor stream rather than frank retention at first presentation
  • Sexual dysfunction: in men, this typically presents as erectile dysfunction; in women, as reduced genital sensation or anorgasmia. Both sexes can experience reduced perineal/genital sensation more generally. This is worth asking about directly as patients frequently won't volunteer it unprompted
  • Variable lower limb motor and sensory deficit, may be asymmetric

docs.title: Examination findings, and how much to trust them
docs.text:
Digital rectal examination (DRE) of anal tone is close to universally taught as a mandatory part of CES assessment. On the current evidence, testing anal tone specifically does not meaningfully help rule CES in or out. Sensitivity is poor. One large study found no statistical association with MRI-confirmed compression and DRE findings. The practice is retained in guidance and training mainly for documentation and medicolegal reasons, not because it performs well as a diagnostic test. That distinction, between "expected practice" and "clinically useful test," is worth trainees noticing and weighing up for themselves rather than assuming the two are the same thing.

Anal tone, a motor finding, is not the same as perianal sensory testing performed during the PR examination. Conflating the two risks trainees concluding the whole PR exam is worthless, which the evidence doesn't support.

Finding What the evidence shows Anal tone (DRE) A systematic review and meta-analysis of 6 studies, 741 patients found sensitivity ranging only 0.23-0.53 across studies, concluding DRE of anal tone "carries a high risk of false reassurance" and is "not recommended in any clinical setting." A separate study of 1005 patients found no association between abnormal anal tone and MRI-confirmed compression at all Reduced perianal sensation A distinct, sensory finding, tested separately from tone during the same examination. Mixed but generally more favourable evidence than tone: the same 1005-patient study reported sensitivity 60%, specificity 68% for perianal sensation; an older meta-analysis by Fairbank et al. reported high specificity for perianal sensory loss.

A normal panel together still carries some weight

Testing tone in isolation performs poorly, but there is some evidence that a fully normal panel, tone, perianal sensation, anal squeeze and bulbocavernosus reflex all together, carries more reassurance than any single component alone. This is a different claim from "anal tone is individually useful", and it's easy to blur the two without noticing, worth holding this loosely given how thin the underlying evidence for the combined-panel claim still is.

| Bulbocavernosus reflex (BCR) | One small study reported 100% sensitivity and 100% specificity, the best-performing single sign identified, but based on limited numbers and a test many non-specialists are unfamiliar with | | Anal squeeze | One study reported sensitivity 29%, specificity 96% |

Teaching point: no single examination finding, including DRE, reliably rules CES in or out. A normal PR exam should not be used to defer imaging or discharge a patient with a genuinely concerning history. Despite this, DRE remains widely regarded as part of a comprehensive assessment and is commonly expected from a medicolegal perspective, this tension is picked up again in the Medicolegal note below.

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
Emergency MRI is the investigation of choice and should not be delayed for a PR examination result if MRI access is the rate-limiting step. The GIRFT-recommended protocol is specific and worth getting right: T2-weighted sagittal and axial lumbar spine first, and only if this is negative for a cause of the patient's symptoms, add a T2-weighted sagittal (not axial) scan of the cervical and thoracic spine to look for a more rostral cause of cord or root compression. This staged approach, rather than a full whole-spine axial protocol from the outset, keeps scan time down to around 15 minutes rather than 30, which matters when MRI capacity is the bottleneck (GIRFT-aligned QI project, 2025).

Controversy: can a bladder scan safely rule out CES?

Post-void residual (PVR) bladder scanning has been promoted as a more objective adjunct than physical examination, but how much weight to put on it is genuinely contested.

  • Venkatesan et al., Spine 2019: a PVR <200 mL carried a negative predictive value of around 97%, and gave a CES probability of only 3.6%; a PVR >200 mL raised the probability of CES to 43%.
  • Katzouraki et al., Bone & Joint Journal 2020: a prospective study of 260 patients found a PVR cut-off of ≥200 mL was significantly more accurate than clinical red flags for predicting cauda equina compression on subsequent MRI, sensitivity 94%, specificity 66.8%, negative predictive value 98.7%.
  • Audit of 50 medicolegal CES cases, International Orthopaedics 2022: more than half (54%) had a PVR of ≤200 mL despite confirmed CES, all classified as CESI, all proceeding to emergency decompression, directly challenging the safety of using a <200 mL PVR to delay or avoid emergency MRI.
  • Working position: PVR is a useful adjunct in a patient without other red flags, not a standalone test that can safely override clinical concern or a convincing history. A normal PVR must not be used to override the GIRFT pathway or clinical suspicion, and should never on its own delay imaging in a patient who otherwise looks like CES. This is worth stating as a direct take-home rather than something merely implied by the numbers above.

docs.title: Differential diagnosis
docs.text:
Conus medullaris syndrome, mechanical low back pain, peripheral neuropathy, and functional neurological disorder are the key differentials.

The last of these is a genuinely important and under-taught area. In the same prospective cohort referenced above (Hoeritzauer et al., Neurology 2021), patients with "scan-negative" CES (normal MRI) had significantly more positive clinical signs of functional neurological disorder (68% vs 11% in scan-positive patients), and history or presence of functional symptoms, normal patellar reflexes, more severe pain, and panic attack at presentation were all associated with a scan-negative outcome. This doesn't change the emergency imaging pathway, a normal MRI is still required to exclude compression, but it's worth knowing that a substantial proportion of the suspected-CES caseload will turn out to have no cauda equina compression at all, some of these patients will have a functional neurological disorder, and they still need honest, careful follow-up rather than being written off once compression is excluded.

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Surgical decompression is indicated for CESI, CESR and CES-Complete in radiographically confirmed CES. Timing is only one operative consideration, the approach itself (typically posterior microdiscectomy or laminectomy, depending on the level and pathology causing compression) is chosen for whichever route achieves the most effective decompression of the compressed roots, not dictated by timing alone. The GIRFT pathway classifies CESI surgery as an NCEPOD E1/E2 emergency, time-sensitive and life-changing though not immediately life-threatening, and states any reason for delay should be documented. Timing of surgery for CESR is explicitly left to the operating surgeon's discretion, but the pathway notes that even though CESR carries a poorer prognosis, around 70% of these patients will still benefit from decompression, worth knowing since it's easy to assume a late presentation isn't worth operating on.

Controversy: does timing within 24-48h actually change outcome?

This is a good exam topic precisely because it resists a clean answer, and because two groups of authors have published a direct, on-the-record disagreement about it.

  • A prospective cohort of 33 patients (Qureshi & Sell, European Spine Journal 2007) found no significant difference in outcome between <24h, 24-48h, and >48h groups. Continence status at presentation, not time-to-surgery, was the dominant prognostic factor. The authors argued this challenged assumptions embedded in "surgical and medico-legal thinking."
  • This was directly challenged in a published letter to the editor by Todd (2009), with the authors' reply, drawing on his own earlier work arguing timing does matter. The disagreement was never resolved in the literature.
  • A pooled systematic-review logistic regression of 322 patients found sensory/motor deficit, urinary incontinence and rectal dysfunction more likely to improve with decompression within 48h, though the review is explicit that timing definitions vary so much between studies that pooling them is itself methodologically shaky.
  • The systematic review by Mustafa et al. (2023) found only 14.5% of 110 studies used an established CES definition at all, and timing definitions were similarly inconsistent, part of why this question remains genuinely unsettled rather than just old.

Working position, worth remembering above everything else on this page:

  • Decompress as soon as feasible rather than promising patients a specific hour-count.
  • Continence status at presentation is the most consistently reported prognostic factor across current observational studies, more so than time to surgery.
  • Document clinical reasoning explicitly, given how disproportionately this condition features in litigation.

docs.title: Complications
docs.text:
• Persistent bladder/bowel/sexual dysfunction, more likely with longer-standing or more severe compression at presentation.
  • Failure to improve despite timely decompression, a recognised and difficult outcome, worth setting as a realistic expectation rather than assuming timely surgery guarantees recovery.

docs.title: Prognosis
docs.text: In the largest UK prospective cohort (Woodfield et al., 621 participants), catheterisation for urinary retention was required pre-operatively in 31% of patients; by discharge this had fallen to 13%, indicating meaningful but incomplete recovery is the typical postoperative course, not full and immediate resolution. Continence status at presentation remains the single most consistently reported prognostic factor across current observational studies, more so than time to surgery, though it's worth noting the underlying evidence base is observational rather than high-quality controlled data.

docs.title: Medicolegal note
docs.text:
CES is disproportionately represented in NHS litigation. NHS Resolution received 827 claims relating to CES between January 2008 and December 2018, of which 340 were settled with damages at a total cost of £186,134,049. The GIRFT pathway itself states that 23% of all litigated claims for spinal surgery in England relate to CES. Delays in recognition in primary care and delays in MRI access, rather than surgical delay once CES is diagnosed, are the most commonly cited contributing factors.

This is where the DRE tension from the examination section above actually matters in practice: a normal anal tone finding has weak diagnostic value, but omitting DRE entirely from documentation is itself viewed unfavourably in litigation, since it looks like an incomplete assessment regardless of what the finding would have shown. The pragmatic position is to examine and document thoroughly, including DRE, while not treating a reassuring DRE as a reason to avoid or delay imaging.

There is early evidence the GIRFT pathway itself is improving care: an independent study from the Northern Care Alliance, published in The Neuroradiology Journal, found that after implementing GIRFT guidance, hospital admissions for suspected CES fell from 57% to 43% of cases, with same-day scanning rates rising from 53% to 62%, and no change in the proportion of scans that actually identified CES (4% in both periods), meaning the improvement in efficiency didn't come at the cost of missed diagnoses (Thiagarajah et al.).

docs.title: Evidence base
docs.text: Study Key finding GIRFT, National Suspected Cauda Equina Syndrome Pathway, March 2026 The UK national standard of care pathway, covering symptoms, initial management, bladder scanning, radiology, surgery and post-operative care. Referenced throughout this page. Thiagarajah et al., GIRFT guidance reduces CES-related admissions without negatively impacting diagnosis Independent single-centre evaluation. Admissions fell 57% to 43% after GIRFT implementation, same-day scanning rose 53% to 62%, CES detection rate unchanged (4%). Lavy C et al., Cauda equina syndrome-a practical guide to definition and classification, International Orthopaedics 2022 Reviewed 212 papers, found 17 different definitions of CES in use. The two-stage CESI/CESR classification is the most widely used and has prognostic value. Definition and surgical timing in CES: an updated systematic review, 2023 110 studies, 52,008 patients. Only 14.5% used an established CES definition. Timing definitions similarly inconsistent across the literature. Fraser S et al., Cauda equina syndrome: a literature review of its definition and clinical presentation, Archives of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation 2009 One of the most commonly used named CES definitions in the literature (the "Fraser criteria"). Tabrah J et al., Can digital rectal examination be used to detect cauda equina compression? Systematic review and meta-analysis, Musculoskeletal Science and Practice 2022 6 studies, 741 patients. No association found between abnormal anal tone and MRI-confirmed cauda equina compression. PVR >200 mL: 43% CES probability. Katzouraki G et al., A prospective study of the role of bladder scanning and post-void residual volume measurement, Bone & Joint Journal 2020 260 patients. Scan-negative patients had significantly more functional neurological disorder features (68% vs 11%). Results pending.

Further reading

Sell (2007), with authors' reply. European Spine Journal. 2009;18(9):1391-1393. The published disagreement referenced in the timing controversy box, useful primary reading for understanding how contested this remains among specialists, not just a settled historical debate.

docs.title: Spinal Epidural Abscess
docs.text:
Curriculum mapping

T&O curriculum: Acute and chronic infections of the spine, Basic Science > Pathology (p.52, level 2/4/4). Infections e.g. tuberculosis, Critical Conditions (p.53, level 1/4/4). Also listed as "Spinal infections (adult or child)" in the Appendix 3 master critical conditions list (p.89).

See also: Pyogenic Spinal Infection for the broader, non-emergency infection picture, Cauda Equina Syndrome for the closest presentation this gets mistaken for, and Metastatic Spinal Cord Compression for the equivalent compressive emergency from a different cause.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: Spinal epidural abscess (SEA) is a suppurative infection within the spinal epidural space causing cord or cauda equina compromise through a combination of direct mechanical compression, thrombophlebitis, and vascular compromise. It is rare, roughly 0.2-2 cases per 10,000 hospital admissions in the largest meta-analysis of the condition, but the incidence has been rising, attributed to an ageing population with more comorbidity, rising rates of intravenous drug use, and increasing numbers of spinal injections and instrumented procedures.

docs.title: Aetiology and risk factors
docs.text:
Staphylococcus aureus accounts for the large majority of cases. Spread is most commonly haematogenous (from a distant focus such as skin or soft tissue infection, urinary tract infection, or an infected line), but contiguous spread from adjacent discitis/osteomyelitis and direct inoculation (epidural injection, spinal surgery, epidural catheter) both occur.

Recognised risk factors: diabetes mellitus, intravenous drug use, immunosuppression, chronic renal or hepatic disease, indwelling vascular catheters, and any recent spinal intervention. In one case-control study, one or more of these risk factors was present in 98% of confirmed SEA patients versus 21% of controls, arguably a more useful screening question in a patient with new back pain than waiting for the classic triad below.

docs.title: Natural history: Heusner's staging
docs.text:
Heusner's 1948 description of the natural history remains the standard teaching framework, a four-stage progression:

  1. Back pain at the level of the abscess
  2. Nerve root pain radiating from that level
  3. Motor weakness, sensory deficit, and bladder/bowel dysfunction
  4. Paralysis

Progression through these stages can take hours in fulminant cases or evolve over days to weeks in more indolent presentations, this variability is part of why the diagnosis is so often missed early.

docs.title: Clinical presentation, and the triad myth
docs.text:
The "classic triad" of back pain, fever, and neurological deficit is taught almost universally as the way to recognise SEA. It is a genuinely poor screening tool, and this is worth knowing explicitly rather than assuming the textbook description reflects how patients actually present.

  • A landmark meta-analysis of 915 patients established the figures most subsequent reviews still cite: the full triad has a sensitivity of only around 7.9%, though when it is present, it is highly specific (99.2%) for SEA.
  • More recent cohorts confirm this hasn't changed: a study of 457 patients found the classic triad present in only 10% at diagnosis, and an emergency department case-control study found it in just 13% of confirmed cases (versus 1% of controls with similar presentations who did not have SEA).
  • Back pain is by far the most consistent individual feature, present in roughly two-thirds to 90% of cases depending on the series. Fever is present in a minority to around two-thirds of cases across different cohorts, and is not a reliable rule-out on its own.

Teaching point: don't wait for all three features of the triad before considering SEA. A patient with new or worsening back pain and any recognised risk factor deserves the diagnosis actively considered, and inflammatory markers checked, well before fever or a neurological deficit appears. Diagnostic delay is consistently reported as the leading driver of poor outcomes and of medicolegal claims relating to SEA, this is picked up again below.

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
• Gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the whole spine is the investigation of choice and the only modality that reliably defines the extent of the abscess and degree of cord compression. Non-contrast MRI can miss or underestimate an abscess, worth being specific that gadolinium is required when requesting the scan.
  • Inflammatory markers (ESR and CRP) are elevated in the large majority of confirmed cases (ESR above 20mm/h in around 94% of cases in one series) and are far more sensitive than a raised white cell count, though neither is specific to SEA. Useful as a triage/screening adjunct in a patient with risk factors and pain, not as a diagnostic test in isolation.
  • Blood cultures, positive in a large majority of cases given how commonly SEA arises from bacteraemia, should be sent before starting empirical antibiotics wherever the patient's clinical state allows.
  • Image-guided or intra-operative biopsy for organism identification where blood cultures are negative or the diagnosis is otherwise unclear, important for targeting the prolonged antibiotic course that follows.

docs.title: Differential diagnosis
docs.text: Discitis/vertebral osteomyelitis without epidural extension, mechanical back pain, metastatic spinal cord compression, transverse myelitis, and other causes of acute cord or cauda equina compression.

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Controversy: antibiotics alone, or decompression?

This mirrors the CES and bilateral facet dislocation timing debates in structure, if not in specifics: the overall evidence doesn't clearly favour one default strategy, but specific patient factors change the answer considerably.

  • The largest systematic review and meta-analysis to date (808 patients, 20 studies) found no statistically significant difference in neurological outcome between surgical and conservative management overall. However, delayed surgery was significantly associated with a worse outcome, an important nuance: the finding isn't "surgery doesn't matter," it's "if surgery is going to happen, doing it promptly matters a great deal."
  • Predictors consistently associated with failure of antibiotics-alone management include: significant or progressive neurological deficit at presentation, MRSA as the causative organism, age over 65, diabetes, markedly elevated CRP, and leukocytosis. One larger series specifically identified motor deficit at presentation, a pathological or compression fracture at the affected level, active malignancy, diabetes, sensory change, and a dorsal (posterior) location of the abscess as independent predictors of failure.
  • In practice, a substantial proportion of patients started on antibiotics alone will still end up needing surgery, one series reported 41% of conservatively-managed patients required delayed surgical intervention.
  • Working position: a neurologically intact patient without the risk factors above can reasonably be trialled on targeted antibiotics with very close neurological monitoring and a low threshold for repeat MRI, but any neurological deficit at presentation, or any deterioration during a trial of antibiotics, is an indication for urgent surgical decompression. This isn't a philosophical choice between two equally valid strategies, it's active risk-stratification of an individual patient.

Antibiotic therapy: empirical broad-spectrum cover (typically including MRSA cover pending sensitivities) is started promptly once cultures are secured, then rationalised to the causative organism once known. Courses are prolonged, commonly of the order of six to eight weeks of targeted therapy, guided by microbiology/infectious diseases input and clinical/inflammatory marker response rather than a fixed duration.

docs.title: Prognosis
docs.text: Prognosis correlates directly with pre-treatment neurological status and the duration of any deficit before decompression, full neurological recovery is substantially less likely once paralysis has been present for more than 24 hours, one of the strongest arguments for urgent recognition and action rather than a specific hour-based cut-off. Outcomes are worse in the elderly, in diabetics, and in MRSA infection.

docs.title: Evidence base
docs.text: Study Key finding Reihsaus E et al., Spinal epidural abscess: a meta-analysis of 915 patients, Neurosurgical Review 2000 The foundational meta-analysis. Classic triad sensitivity 7.9%, specificity 99.2%. Diabetes the most common risk factor. Heusner AP, Nontuberculous spinal epidural infections, New England Journal of Medicine 1948 Original description of the four-stage natural history (back pain, radicular pain, weakness, paralysis) still used as the standard teaching framework. Davis DP et al., The clinical presentation and impact of diagnostic delays on emergency department patients with spinal epidural abscess Case-control study. Classic triad present in 13% of SEA patients vs 1% of controls. One or more risk factors present in 98% vs 21%. ESR more sensitive and specific than white cell count. Diagnostic Delays Are Common, and Classic Presentations Are Rare in Spinal Epidural Abscess, 2024 457 patients. Classic triad present in only 10%. Over two-thirds had at least one prior healthcare visit before diagnosis. Wang TY et al., Neurological Outcomes After Surgical or Conservative Management of Spontaneous Spinal Epidural Abscesses, Clinical Spine Surgery 2019 Systematic review and meta-analysis, 808 patients, 20 studies. No significant difference in outcome between surgical and conservative management overall; delayed surgery specifically associated with worse outcome. Spernovasilis N et al., Characteristics and predictors of outcome of spontaneous spinal epidural abscesses treated conservatively Conservative-management cohort. Severe neurological deficit and MRSA infection were the strongest predictors of treatment failure. Sharfman ZT et al., Spinal Epidural Abscess: A Review of Presentation, Management, and Medicolegal Implications Review specifically addressing the medicolegal dimension of diagnostic delay in SEA.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Reihsaus E, Waldbaur H, Seeling W. Spinal epidural abscess: a meta-analysis of 915 patients. Neurosurgical Review. 2000;23(4):175-204.
  2. Heusner AP. Nontuberculous spinal epidural infections. New England Journal of Medicine. 1948;239:845-854.
  3. Davis DP, Wold RM, Patel RJ, et al. The clinical presentation and impact of diagnostic delays on emergency department patients with spinal epidural abscess. Journal of Emergency Medicine. 2004.
  4. Diagnostic Delays Are Common, and Classic Presentations Are Rare in Spinal Epidural Abscess. 2024.
  5. Wang TY, Harward SC, Tsvankin V, et al. Neurological Outcomes After Surgical or Conservative Management of Spontaneous Spinal Epidural Abscesses: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Data From 1980 Through 2016. Clinical Spine Surgery. 2019;32(1):18-29.
  6. Spernovasilis N, Demetriou S, Bachlitzanaki M, et al. Characteristics and predictors of outcome of spontaneous spinal epidural abscesses treated conservatively: A retrospective cohort study in a referral center.
  7. Sharfman ZT, Gelfand Y, Shah P, et al. Spinal Epidural Abscess: A Review of Presentation, Management, and Medicolegal Implications. Asian Spine Journal. 2020;14(5):742-753.

docs.title: Infection
docs.text:
Pyogenic and granulomatous infection of the spine.

  • Pyogenic Spinal Infection, including discitis and epidural abscess
  • Spinal Tuberculosis

Infection is a named critical condition in the T&O curriculum (Appendix 3), and is one of the differentials that must be actively excluded whenever a patient presents with new back pain and systemic upset, worth cross-referencing from the relevant degenerative pages once those carry a red-flags section.

docs.title: Inflammatory
docs.text:
Rheumatological and inflammatory conditions with spinal manifestations, an area where the spine can be affected quite differently to peripheral joints and where the trauma risk is easy to underestimate.

  • Rheumatoid Cervical Spine, atlantoaxial and subaxial instability
  • Ankylosing Spondylitis, including the fracture risk in an ossified spine

Both conditions are named in the T&O curriculum's critical conditions list under "complications of inflammatory spinal conditions", worth stating plainly on both pages once written, as it's an easy critical condition to forget sits here rather than under Trauma.

docs.title: Lumbar Spine
docs.text:
Degenerative conditions of L1-S1. For lumbar trauma see Trauma, for isthmic spondylolisthesis in the paediatric or young adult population see Deformity.

  • Lumbar Disc Herniation & Sciatica
  • Lumbar Spinal Stenosis & Degenerative Spondylolisthesis, includes the fusion-vs-decompression-alone evidence, probably the best-evidenced controversy on the whole site
  • Revision Lumbar Spine Surgery

Cauda equina syndrome can arise from lumbar pathology covered on these pages, but it's kept on its own page under Emergencies given how time-critical it is, worth linking to from here rather than duplicating.

docs.title: Lumbar Disc Herniation & Sciatica
docs.text:
Curriculum mapping

Non-operative treatment of low back pain and sciatica, Treatments > Non-operative (p.54, level 1/4/4). Compressive conditions, Treatments > Operative (p.53, level 1/4/4). Skills: discectomy open/micro/endoscopic (p.67, level 0/2/4).

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Content outline drafted, full clinical write-up pending. See the site plan document for the full content brief.

docs.title: Evidence base
docs.text: Study Key finding Peul WC et al., Surgery versus prolonged conservative treatment for sciatica, NEJM 2007 Early surgery gave faster relief of leg pain than prolonged conservative care, but outcomes converged by one year. Weinstein JN et al., SPORT trial, JAMA 2006 As-treated analysis favoured surgery, but very high crossover between arms limited the intention-to-treat result. Liu C et al., Surgical versus non-surgical treatment for sciatica: systematic review and meta-analysis of RCTs, BMJ 2023 Evidence favouring surgery rated low-certainty overall; benefit over conservative care is short-to-medium term, barely detectable at one year.

docs.title: Lumbar Spinal Stenosis & Degenerative Spondylolisthesis
docs.text:
Curriculum mapping

T&O curriculum: The aging spine and degenerative disease, Basic Science > Pathology (p.52, level 2/4/4). Compressive conditions (radicular/stenotic/myelopathic), Treatments > Operative (p.53, level 1/4/4).

Skills: Decompression lumbar spine without fusion (p.67, level 0/2/4); decompression with fusion +/- fixation (p.67, level 0/1/4).

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: Degenerative narrowing of the spinal canal, lateral recess, or neural foramen, most commonly from facet joint hypertrophy and ligamentum flavum thickening, causing neurogenic claudication and/or radiculopathy.

docs.title: Clinical presentation
docs.text:
• Neurogenic claudication: leg pain/heaviness worse on walking and standing, relieved by flexion (leaning on a trolley, sitting)
  • Distinguish from vascular claudication: neurogenic pain typically eases with flexion regardless of continued walking; vascular claudication eases only with rest
  • Radiological severity correlates poorly with symptom severity, treat the patient, not the scan

docs.title: Investigation
docs.text: MRI lumbar spine is first line. Note the grade of central/lateral recess/foraminal stenosis but weigh this against the clinical picture.

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
• Conservative: physiotherapy, weight management, analgesia, epidural steroid injection for symptom control
  • Surgical: decompression (laminectomy, unilateral laminotomy for bilateral decompression, or less invasive approaches)

Controversy: does degenerative spondylolisthesis need fusion alongside decompression?

This is the best-evidenced controversy in the entire spine syllabus, three major RCTs now broadly converge.

Study Design Finding Försth et al. (Swedish Spinal Stenosis Study), NEJM 2016 247 patients, decompression alone vs decompression + fusion No significant difference in ODI at 2 or 5 years. Fusion group had longer hospital stay, more bleeding, higher cost. Ghogawala et al., NEJM 2016 66 patients, grade I spondylolisthesis only Some outcomes favoured fusion, diverged from Försth, fuelled ongoing debate about entry criteria. NORDSTEN-DS trial, NEJM 2021 Noninferiority design Decompression alone was noninferior to decompression with instrumented fusion. Bone Joint J 2022, SSSS 2-year MRI follow-up Radiological outcomes Decompression alone did not increase restenosis or proximal adjacent-level stenosis vs fusion.

Working position: routine fusion adds cost, blood loss and hospital stay without a consistent functional benefit for straightforward degenerative spondylolisthesis with stenosis. Reserve fusion for frank instability, deformity, or recurrent stenosis.

docs.title: Miscellaneous
docs.text:
The reference material that underpins the condition pages rather than a condition in its own right, biomechanics, instrumentation, and the general complications of spinal surgery that don't belong to one specific pathology.

Planned pages:

  • Spinal biomechanics and instability, the concepts referenced across the Trauma and Deformity sections rather than repeated on each page
  • Spinal instrumentation, screws, rods, cages, and interbody devices, what they are before diving into when to use them
  • Complications of spinal surgery in general (surgical site infection, neurological deterioration, dural tear), as distinct from the complications specific to one operation covered on that operation's own page
  • Post-operative rehabilitation principles

Like Radiology, this section is reference material rather than a single evidence-driven topic, so it's a lower priority for literature searching and a higher priority for clear original diagrams.

docs.title: Paediatric Spine
docs.text:
This is a hub page, not a separate set of conditions. Paediatric spinal pathology is written up on the pages where it clinically belongs (Deformity, Trauma, Infection), and linked from here so someone approaching the topic "by age" rather than "by pathology" can still find everything in one place.

Deformity

  • Adolescent Idiopathic Scoliosis
  • Early Onset Scoliosis
  • Spondylolysis & Isthmic Spondylolisthesis

Trauma

  • Paediatric spinal trauma has distinct injury patterns and clearance criteria from adult trauma, page pending under Trauma.

Infection

  • Paediatric discitis has a different typical presentation (younger children, refusal to weight-bear) to adult pyogenic infection, worth its own short section on the Pyogenic Spinal Infection page once written rather than a separate page here.

If a topic is genuinely paediatric-specific throughout (no meaningful adult equivalent), it can live directly under this section instead, use judgement rather than forcing everything into the adult pages.

docs.title: Radiology
docs.text:
Not yet built. BOFAS's Hyperbook runs this as a numbered series of short imaging-focused pages (normal anatomy, classic pathological appearances, measurement techniques) rather than folding imaging into every condition page.

Planned scope:

  1. Normal radiographic anatomy, cervical, thoracic, lumbar
  2. Measurement techniques referenced elsewhere on the site (Cobb angle, Pavlov ratio, SINS score, Bilsky grading), collected here as a single reference point rather than re-explained on every page that uses them
  3. MRI basics for trainees who haven't done a radiology attachment, sequence types, what "T2 hyperintensity" actually means
  4. Classic appearances by pathology (OPLL on CT, discitis on MRI, metastatic disease patterns)

This section is lower priority for literature searching than the condition pages, it's mostly stable anatomical and technique knowledge, and higher priority for good original annotated images, which is a different kind of authoring task.

docs.title: Thoracic Spine
docs.text:
The smallest section on the site by volume, and genuinely so, thoracic pathology is less common than cervical or lumbar disease, partly because the rib cage adds substantial stability that the rest of the spine doesn't have.

  • Thoracic Disc Herniation

Thoracic trauma and thoracic deformity are covered under Trauma and Deformity respectively rather than here, this section is specifically for thoracic degenerative disease.

docs.title: Osteoporotic Vertebral Fractures & Vertebroplasty
docs.text:
Curriculum mapping

Management of spinal fractures including osteoporotic fractures, Treatments > Non-operative (p.54, level 1/4/4). Skills: vertebroplasty/balloon kyphoplasty (p.67, level 0/1/4).

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Content outline drafted, full clinical write-up pending.

docs.title: Evidence base
docs.text:
Controversy: does vertebroplasty actually work?

Five major blinded trials give genuinely conflicting answers depending on fracture chronicity and comparator.

Study Design Finding VAPOUR, Clark W et al., Lancet 2016 Double-blind, placebo-controlled, acute (<6 week) fractures Positive for vertebroplasty. VERTOS IV, Firanescu C et al., BMJ 2018 Randomised, sham-controlled, acute fractures Negative, no benefit over sham. VERTOS V, Radiology 2023 Active-control (not sham), chronic (>3 month) fractures Vertebroplasty superior to anaesthetic infiltration at 12 months.

docs.title: Tumour
docs.text:
Primary and metastatic disease of the spine and spinal cord.

  • Primary Spinal Tumours
  • Metastatic Spinal Cord Compression, a critical condition, includes the NOMS framework and the shift from aggressive resection to separation surgery plus radiosurgery

MSCC is the tumour-related emergency trainees are most likely to be tested on and most likely to encounter on call, start there if you're new to this section.

docs.title: Metastatic Spinal Cord Compression
docs.text:
Curriculum mapping

Critical Condition, Spine module (p.53) and Appendix 3 (p.89), level 2/4/4. Principles of management of tumours around the spine, Treatments > Operative (p.53, level 1/4/4).

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Content outline drafted, full clinical write-up pending, including NOMS framework, SINS score, and Bilsky grading reference tables.

docs.title: Evidence base
docs.text: Study Key finding Patchell RA et al., landmark RCT, surgery+radiotherapy vs radiotherapy alone 84% of the surgical arm remained ambulatory vs a significantly lower proportion in the radiotherapy-alone arm. Laufer, Bilsky et al., NOMS framework and separation surgery; reviewed in Neuro-Oncology Advances 2024 Bilsky grade 0-1c disease often treated with SRS alone; grade 2-3 disease uses separation surgery (limited decompression) paired with SRS. International Multidisciplinary Consensus Group for MSCC, Lancet Oncology 2025/2026 Recent consensus effort to standardise definitions/reporting/endpoints across MSCC trials.
